name: Suoi Giang Wild Ancient Tea, Yen Bai, Vietnam (~1400m)

staple_foods.name: green tea (Camellia sinensis)

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 16.6-20.7 g/100g dry leaf total catechins [Suoi Giang wild/ancient-tree green teas, 7 samples at ~1400m — Nguyen et al. 2023 Antioxidants 12:1003; PMC10142074; sample totals: 166.1, 166.2, 167.2, 172.1, 172.6, 192.9, 207.0 mg/g DM]
key_flavor_bioactives.caffeine_concentration: 2.5-3.5 g/100g dry leaf [wild-tree green tea — Nguyen et al. 2023; higher caffeine in wild assamica-type trees]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 1.0-2.0 g/100g dry leaf as theanine+glutamate [highland tea — Nguyen et al. 2023; wild-tree tea notably higher theanine than cultivated]

staple_foods.cultivar: Camellia sinensis var. assamica (wild/ancient Shan Tuyet trees, >200 years old)
staple_foods.notes: Suoi Giang commune, Van Chan District, Yen Bai Province, northern Vietnam — situated at ~1400m on mountainside forests with trees over 200-300 years old. The 7 wild/ancient-tree (Shan Tuyet) green tea samples measured by Nguyen et al. (2023) via HPLC-DAD gave total catechins of 166.1, 166.2, 167.2, 172.1, 172.6, 192.9, and 207.0 mg/g DM — range 16.6-20.7 g/100g DM. This is minimally processed green tea (the fresh leaf is steamed and sun-dried or pan-fired in the Shan Tuyet tradition), not shade-grown and not oxidized. IMPORTANT CONFOUND: the Suoi Giang trees are var. assamica wild-types rather than cultivated clones, so elevated catechins here partly reflect genetic background, not only altitude. Compare RSU-56 (Da Lat cultivated clones, ~1500m, 11.5-17.0 g/100g) to isolate domestication vs. wild-type effects. The upper range of RSU-57 (~20.7 g/100g) overlaps the lower range of RSU-20 Darjeeling (18.0-22.0 g/100g), providing a coherent across-region altitude gradient anchor. Mong ethnic community harvests using traditional stone-tumbling processing; no industrial processing.
data_sources: Nguyen et al. (2023) — Characteristics and Relationships between Total Polyphenol and Flavonoid Contents, Antioxidant Capacities, and the Content of Caffeine, Gallic Acid, and Major Catechins in Wild/Ancient and Cultivated Teas in Vietnam, Antioxidants 12(5):1003 [PMC10142074]

notes: Suoi Giang commune, Yen Bai Province, northern Vietnam (~1400m). Metamorphic highland soils, cool montane climate (average 8-10°C cooler than valley floor). Home to over 85,000 wild ancient Shan Tuyet tea trees (Camellia sinensis var. assamica) >200 years old — among the largest surviving population of wild-type C. sinensis in Vietnam. This RSU is paired with RSU-56 (Da Lat cultivated teas, ~1500m) in the Vietnamese altitude experiment and with RSU-20 (Darjeeling, 2000m) and RSU-21 (Assam, 100m) in the broader altitude-catechin natural experiment. The wild-type genetic background elevates catechins independently of altitude — documented in Nguyen et al. 2023 who showed wild/ancient trees consistently exceeded cultivated trees at matched altitude. The Hmong (Mong) ethnic community here is the primary steward of these trees and uses traditional minimal-processing green tea methods.
